title: Ecological Interactions
subtitle: Food chains and webs, energy flow, habitats, adaptation and interdependence.

studyGuide.heading: Key ecology words
studyGuide.html:
• Habitat — the place where an organism lives.
  • Population — all the members of one species in an area.
  • Community — all the populations (different species) living together.
  • Ecosystem — the community plus its non-living surroundings (water, soil, light).

studyGuide.heading: Food chains
studyGuide.html:
A food chain shows how energy passes from one organism to the next. The arrow means 'is eaten by' (energy flows in the arrow's direction).

grass → rabbit → fox

  • Producer — makes its own food by photosynthesis (always a plant; the start of the chain).
  • Primary consumer — eats the producer (a herbivore).
  • Secondary / tertiary consumer — eats other consumers (carnivores).
  • Predator — hunts and eats other animals; prey — the animal that is eaten.

diagram.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 440 70' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.t{font:bold 12px Nunito;fill:#1f2a44}.s{font:9px Nunito;fill:#6b7790}.a{stroke:#2b9d6b;stroke-width:2.5;fill:none;marker-end:url(#ar)}</style><defs><marker id='ar' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 z' fill='#2b9d6b'/></marker></defs><text class='t' x='10' y='35'>grass</text><text class='s' x='10' y='48'>producer</text><line class='a' x1='55' y1='30' x2='115' y2='30'/><text class='t' x='120' y='35'>rabbit</text><text class='s' x='120' y='48'>primary</text><line class='a' x1='175' y1='30' x2='235' y2='30'/><text class='t' x='240' y='35'>stoat</text><text class='s' x='240' y='48'>secondary</text><line class='a' x1='290' y1='30' x2='350' y2='30'/><text class='t' x='355' y='35'>fox</text><text class='s' x='355' y='48'>tertiary</text></svg>
diagram.caption: Arrows show energy flow (is eaten by). It always starts with a producer.

studyGuide.mnemonic: The arrow points to the eater — energy flows into whatever the arrow points at.

studyGuide.heading: Food webs and energy loss
studyGuide.html:
• A food web is several food chains linked together, showing that animals eat more than one thing.
  • Only about 10% of energy passes to the next level; the rest is lost as heat, movement and waste.
  • That is why food chains are usually short (3–5 links) and there are fewer top predators than producers.

diagram.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 430 178' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.o{fill:#e6f7ef;stroke:#2b9d6b;stroke-width:2}.d{stroke:#2b9d6b;stroke-width:1.5}.n{font:bold 12px Nunito;fill:#1f2a44}.l{font:10px Nunito;fill:#3f6fd1}.w{font:9px Nunito;fill:#e0524d}</style><polygon class='o' points='180,15 305,160 55,160'/><line class='d' x1='150' y1='50' x2='210' y2='50'/><line class='d' x1='115' y1='90' x2='245' y2='90'/><line class='d' x1='85' y1='125' x2='275' y2='125'/><text class='n' x='180' y='41' text-anchor='middle'>10</text><text class='n' x='180' y='76' text-anchor='middle'>100</text><text class='n' x='180' y='112' text-anchor='middle'>1000</text><text class='n' x='180' y='148' text-anchor='middle'>10 000</text><text class='l' x='312' y='41'>top predator</text><text class='l' x='312' y='76'>2nd consumer</text><text class='l' x='312' y='112'>1st consumer</text><text class='l' x='312' y='148'>producers</text><text class='w' x='6' y='150'>~90% energy lost</text><text class='w' x='6' y='162'>at each level (heat,</text><text class='w' x='6' y='174'>movement, waste)</text></svg>
diagram.caption: Energy (in units) falls to about a tenth at each level up — so there is far less at the top, and only a few top predators.

studyGuide.heading: Interdependence — what happens if numbers change
studyGuide.html:
Organisms in a web depend on each other. If one population changes, others are affected:

  • If a predator is removed, its prey increases (no longer eaten)…
  • …then the prey's food source decreases (more mouths eating it).
  • Predator and prey numbers rise and fall in cycles that follow each other.

studyGuide.heading: Adaptation
studyGuide.html:
Organisms have adaptations — features that help them survive in their habitat.

  • Polar bear: thick fur and fat for insulation; white camouflage; large feet to spread weight on snow.
  • Cactus: spines (reduce water loss, deter animals); thick stem to store water; deep/wide roots.
  • Camel: stores fat in hump; can go long without water; long eyelashes against sand.
studyGuide.mnemonic: Adaptation = a feature that gives an animal/plant an advantage to survive (both start with 'ad-').

studyGuide.heading: Sampling and the environment
studyGuide.html:
• A quadrat is a square frame used to count plants/slow animals in a measured area; results are scaled up to estimate the whole habitat.
  • Living things can be affected by pollution (e.g. fertiliser run-off, oil spills).
  • Some species are indicator species — their presence/absence tells us how clean the environment is (e.g. lichen for clean air).

flashcards.back: The place where an organism lives.
All the members of one species living in an area.
All the different populations living together in an area.
A community plus its non-living surroundings (light, water, soil).

flashcards.front: What does the arrow in a food chain mean?
flashcards.back: 'Is eaten by' — energy flows in the direction of the arrow.
An organism (plant) that makes its own food by photosynthesis; starts the chain.
An animal that eats the producer (a herbivore).
Predator hunts and eats; prey is the animal that is eaten.

flashcards.front: How much energy passes to the next level?
flashcards.back: About 10% — the rest is lost as heat, movement and waste.
Because so much energy is lost at each level, there isn't enough for many links.
Its prey increases, then the prey's food source decreases.
A feature that helps an organism survive in its habitat.
Spines (reduce water loss), thick stem to store water, wide/deep roots.

flashcards.front: What is a quadrat used for?
flashcards.back: Counting organisms in a measured area to estimate population size.
A species whose presence/absence shows how polluted an environment is (e.g. lichen).

commonMistakes.title: Drawing food-chain arrows backwards
commonMistakes.detail: The arrow points from the food TO the eater (energy flow), e.g. grass → rabbit, not rabbit → grass.

commonMistakes.title: Starting a food chain with an animal
commonMistakes.detail: Food chains start with a producer (a plant), the source of the energy.

commonMistakes.title: Saying plants are consumers
commonMistakes.detail: Plants are producers — they make their own food by photosynthesis.

commonMistakes.title: Forgetting energy is lost between levels
commonMistakes.detail: Only ~10% passes on; the rest is lost as heat, movement and waste, so chains are short.

commonMistakes.title: Only giving the adaptation, not the reason
commonMistakes.detail: Always link the feature to survival, e.g. 'thick fur TO keep warm in the cold'.

commonMistakes.title: Confusing population and community
commonMistakes.detail: Population = one species; community = all the different species together.

questions.q: In the food chain grass → rabbit → fox, what does the arrow mean?
questions.explanation: The arrow means 'is eaten by' and shows energy flow.
questions.hint: Think about where the energy goes.

questions.q: What is a producer?
options: A plant that makes its own food
questions.explanation: Producers are plants that make food by photosynthesis.
questions.hint: It makes ('produces') its own food.

questions.q: An animal that eats the producer is called a…
questions.explanation: The first consumer (herbivore) is the primary consumer.
questions.hint: First in line after the plant.

questions.q: Which word means 'all the rabbits living in one field'?
questions.explanation: A population is all the members of one species in an area.
questions.hint: One species only.

questions.q: Roughly how much energy passes to the next level of a food chain?
questions.explanation: Only about 10% passes on; the rest is lost as heat, movement and waste.
questions.hint: It's a small fraction.

questions.q: A polar bear's white fur is an adaptation for…
questions.explanation: White fur camouflages the bear against snow when hunting.
questions.hint: How does it stay hidden?

questions.q: Define the word 'habitat'.
questions.modelAnswer: A habitat is the place where an organism lives.
questions.hint: Where does the animal live?
questions.examMistake: Confusing habitat (the place) with ecosystem (community + surroundings).

questions.q: Why must a food chain start with a plant?
questions.modelAnswer: A plant is a producer that makes its own food using light energy in photosynthesis, so it is the source of energy that all the other organisms in the chain depend on.
questions.hint: Where does the energy enter the chain?
questions.examMistake: Forgetting that the plant is the energy source.

questions.q: A food web is…
questions.explanation: A food web links several food chains to show multiple feeding relationships.
questions.hint: More than one chain.

questions.q: Which is an adaptation of a cactus to a dry desert?
options: Spines instead of leaves to reduce water loss
questions.explanation: Spines reduce water loss (and deter animals); a thick stem stores water.
questions.hint: How does it save water?

questions.q: What is a quadrat used for?
questions.modelAnswer: A quadrat is a square frame placed on the ground to count the number of plants or slow-moving animals in a known area, so the population in the whole habitat can be estimated.
questions.hint: It helps you count organisms in a set area.
questions.examMistake: Saying it measures temperature or weather.

questions.q: In a food chain, the predator is the animal that…
questions.explanation: A predator hunts and eats other animals (its prey).
questions.hint: Who does the hunting?

questions.q: In the chain grass → rabbit → fox, explain what would happen to the rabbit and grass populations if all the foxes were removed.
questions.modelAnswer: With no foxes to eat them, the rabbit population would increase. As there are more rabbits, they eat more grass, so the amount of grass would decrease. Eventually the lack of grass could limit the rabbit numbers again.
questions.hint: Remove the predator — what happens to its prey, then the prey's food?
questions.examMistake: Only mentioning the rabbits and forgetting the effect on the grass.

questions.q: Why are there usually far fewer top predators than producers in an ecosystem?
options: Energy is lost at each level, so less is available higher up
questions.explanation: Only ~10% of energy passes up each level, so there is much less energy to support top predators.
questions.hint: Think about energy loss up the chain.

questions.q: Explain the difference between a community and an ecosystem.
questions.modelAnswer: A community is all the populations of different species living together in an area. An ecosystem is that community plus the non-living parts of the environment, such as the soil, water, light and temperature, and the way they all interact.
questions.hint: One adds the non-living surroundings.
questions.examMistake: Using community and ecosystem to mean the same thing.

questions.q: A camel stores fat in its hump. The main benefit is that…
options: It is an energy store for when food is scarce
questions.explanation: The fat is an energy (and water) reserve for the harsh desert where food is rare.
questions.hint: Fat is a store of what?

questions.q: Lichen grows well only in clean air. Explain how lichen can be used as an indicator species.
questions.modelAnswer: Lichen is sensitive to air pollution, so it only grows where the air is clean. By recording where lichen is and is not growing, scientists can tell which areas have clean or polluted air without measuring the pollutants directly — the lichen indicates air quality.
questions.hint: What does its presence or absence tell you?
questions.examMistake: Saying lichen causes clean air rather than indicating it.

questions.q: In a food web, owls eat both mice and shrews. If the mouse population falls, what is most likely?
questions.explanation: A web gives alternatives — owls can switch to eating more shrews.
questions.hint: Webs offer more than one food source.

questions.q: Describe two adaptations of a polar bear and link each to survival in the Arctic.
questions.modelAnswer: A polar bear has thick fur and a layer of fat (blubber) which insulate it and reduce heat loss in the freezing cold. It also has white fur for camouflage against the snow, so it can creep up on prey such as seals without being seen. (Large feet also spread its weight on ice and snow.)
questions.hint: Pick two features and say what each is FOR.
questions.examMistake: Listing features without explaining how they help it survive.

questions.q: Which correctly orders these from smallest to largest?
questions.explanation: One species (population) → all species (community) → community + surroundings (ecosystem).
questions.hint: Start with one species.

questions.q: A farmer sprays insecticide to kill caterpillars that eat crops. Suggest how this could harm birds that eat the caterpillars.
questions.modelAnswer: The birds rely on the caterpillars as a food source, so killing the caterpillars removes the birds' food and the bird population could fall. The insecticide may also build up in the caterpillars and be passed on to the birds, poisoning them. This shows how interfering with one part of a food web affects others.
questions.hint: What do the birds eat, and what happens when it's gone?
questions.examMistake: Forgetting that removing prey affects the predator's food supply.

questions.q: Why do scientists use several quadrats placed randomly rather than one quadrat?
options: To get a representative average and avoid bias
questions.explanation: Several random samples give a fairer, more representative estimate of the whole area.
questions.hint: One sample might be unusual.

questions.q: Explain why energy is lost between levels of a food chain and what this means for the number of organisms at each level.
questions.modelAnswer: At each level, organisms use most of the energy they take in for life processes such as movement, respiration and keeping warm, and energy is lost in waste (faeces) and as heat. Only about 10% is stored in the body and passed on when eaten. Because so little energy reaches the next level, there is less energy to support organisms higher up, so there are fewer organisms (and less total mass) at each higher level — forming a pyramid shape.
questions.hint: Where does the energy go, and how does that shape the numbers?
questions.examMistake: Saying energy 'disappears' rather than being used/lost as heat and waste.

questions.q: Predator and prey populations often rise and fall in repeating cycles. Explain why, using foxes and rabbits.
questions.modelAnswer: When rabbits are plentiful, foxes have lots of food so the fox population grows. More foxes eat more rabbits, so the rabbit population then falls. With fewer rabbits, many foxes starve and the fox population falls. With fewer foxes, rabbits can breed and recover, so rabbit numbers rise again — and the cycle repeats. The predator peak follows the prey peak.
questions.hint: Follow the chain of cause and effect round in a loop.
questions.examMistake: Not explaining the time delay — predator numbers follow prey numbers.

questions.q: A pyramid of numbers for oak tree → caterpillars → birds is an odd shape (narrow at the bottom). Why?
options: One huge tree supports many caterpillars, so the producer level is narrow
questions.explanation: A single large producer (the tree) feeds many small primary consumers, so a number pyramid can be inverted at the base.
questions.hint: How many caterpillars can one tree feed?

questions.q: Fertiliser runs off a farm into a lake, causing algae to grow rapidly then the fish to die. Explain this sequence.
questions.modelAnswer: The fertiliser is rich in nutrients, so algae grow very fast and cover the water surface (an algal bloom). This blocks light from plants below, which die and are broken down by bacteria. The bacteria multiply and use up the dissolved oxygen in respiration, so the fish do not have enough oxygen and die. This process is called eutrophication.
questions.hint: Algae bloom → blocks light → bacteria → oxygen.
questions.examMistake: Saying the fertiliser poisons the fish directly — it's the oxygen loss that kills them.

questions.q: Explain why introducing a new predator (with no natural enemies) to an island can be a disaster for the native species.
questions.modelAnswer: The new predator has no enemies to control its numbers, so its population grows rapidly. It hunts native prey that may not be adapted to escape it, so prey populations crash and some may become extinct. This disrupts the whole food web, affecting other species that depended on those prey, reducing biodiversity.
questions.hint: Nothing controls the predator; the prey can't cope.
questions.examMistake: Forgetting the knock-on effect on the rest of the food web.

questions.q: Why is it more energy-efficient for humans to eat plants directly than to eat animals that ate the plants?
options: Each step up a food chain loses ~90% of the energy, so eating lower down wastes less
questions.explanation: Eating at a lower trophic level avoids the large energy losses between levels.
questions.hint: Energy is lost at each level you go up.

questions.q: Design a method using quadrats to estimate the number of daisies in a school field, and say how you would scale up.
questions.modelAnswer: Place several quadrats (e.g. ten 1 m² quadrats) at random positions across the field to avoid bias — for example using random coordinates. Count the daisies in each quadrat and calculate the mean number per square metre. Then measure the total area of the field and multiply the mean by the total area to estimate the total number of daisies. Using many random quadrats makes the estimate more reliable.
questions.hint: Random samples → mean per m² → × total area.
questions.examMistake: Placing quadrats only where daisies are obvious — that biases the result.

questions.q: A desert plant and an Arctic animal both face harsh conditions. Compare one adaptation each and explain how both are examples of being 'adapted to survive'.
questions.modelAnswer: A desert cactus has a thick, fleshy stem to store water and spines instead of leaves to reduce water loss, helping it survive where water is scarce. An Arctic fox has thick fur and a small body with short ears to reduce heat loss, helping it survive extreme cold. Both have features suited to the specific challenge of their habitat, increasing their chance of survival and reproduction — that is what 'adapted' means.
questions.hint: One conserves water, one conserves heat — both aid survival.
questions.examMistake: Not making clear that adaptation links a feature to surviving the habitat.
